definition: Death of heart muscle due to lack of blood supply
rationale: Myocardial infarction MI or heart attack occurs when coronary artery blockage deprives heart muscle of oxygen causing cell death. The extent of damage depends on which artery is blocked and duration of blockage.

definition: Chest pain from temporary reduced blood flow to heart
rationale: Angina is chest discomfort caused by temporary myocardial ischemia without permanent damage. It typically occurs with exertion and resolves with rest or nitroglycerin unlike MI which causes permanent damage.

definition: Inadequate blood supply to tissue
rationale: Ischemia occurs when blood flow to tissue is insufficient to meet oxygen demands. Prolonged ischemia leads to tissue death. Cardiac ischemia causes angina while prolonged ischemia causes infarction.

definition: Tissue death due to lack of blood supply
rationale: Infarction is death of tissue resulting from prolonged ischemia. When heart muscle dies it is myocardial infarction. When brain tissue dies it is cerebral infarction or stroke.

definition: Abnormal heart rhythm
rationale: Arrhythmia or dysrhythmia is any deviation from normal heart rhythm. They range from benign to life-threatening and include bradycardia tachycardia atrial fibrillation and ventricular fibrillation.

definition: Heart rate slower than 60 beats per minute
rationale: Bradycardia is heart rate below 60 bpm. It may be normal in athletes but symptomatic bradycardia with hypotension or altered mental status requires treatment.

definition: Heart rate faster than 100 beats per minute
rationale: Tachycardia is heart rate above 100 bpm. It may be a normal response to stress fever or exertion or may indicate underlying pathology. Sustained tachycardia reduces cardiac output.

definition: Chaotic disorganized heart rhythm causing cardiac arrest
rationale: Ventricular fibrillation VF is a lethal arrhythmia where the ventricles quiver chaotically producing no cardiac output. It is the most common initial rhythm in sudden cardiac arrest and requires defibrillation.

definition: Complete absence of cardiac electrical activity
rationale: Asystole is the absence of any cardiac electrical activity appearing as a flat line on the monitor. It indicates severe cardiac dysfunction with very poor prognosis. It is not a shockable rhythm.

definition: Heart cannot pump blood effectively causing fluid backup
rationale: CHF occurs when the heart cannot pump effectively causing blood to back up into the lungs left-sided failure or body right-sided failure. Symptoms include dyspnea edema and fatigue.

definition: Fluid accumulation in the lungs
rationale: Pulmonary edema is fluid accumulation in the alveoli impairing gas exchange. It commonly results from left heart failure causing blood to back up into the pulmonary circulation. Symptoms include severe dyspnea and crackles.

definition: Buildup of plaque in artery walls
rationale: Atherosclerosis is progressive buildup of fatty plaques in arterial walls narrowing the lumen and reducing blood flow. It underlies most heart attacks and strokes when plaques rupture and form clots.

definition: Material traveling through bloodstream that blocks a vessel
rationale: An embolus is material usually a blood clot that travels through the bloodstream until it lodges in and blocks a smaller vessel. Pulmonary embolism occurs when emboli block pulmonary arteries.

definition: Blood clot that forms in a blood vessel
rationale: A thrombus is a blood clot that forms and remains in place within a blood vessel. It can obstruct blood flow locally or break off becoming an embolus that travels to block distant vessels.

definition: Inadequate oxygen supply to tissues
rationale: Hypoxia is insufficient oxygen delivery to meet tissue needs. Causes include respiratory failure circulatory failure and anemia. Signs include cyanosis altered mental status and tachycardia.

definition: Low oxygen levels in the blood
rationale: Hypoxemia is abnormally low oxygen tension in arterial blood typically SpO2 below 90% or PaO2 below 60 mmHg. It is measured by pulse oximetry or blood gas analysis and leads to tissue hypoxia.

definition: Elevated carbon dioxide levels in blood
rationale: Hypercapnia is elevated arterial CO2 from inadequate ventilation. It causes acidosis and if severe can cause altered mental status and respiratory failure. It is common in COPD and hypoventilation.

definition: Constriction of airway smooth muscle
rationale: Bronchospasm is contraction of smooth muscle surrounding bronchi causing airway narrowing and increased resistance. It is the primary problem in asthma and responds to bronchodilators.

definition: Air in the pleural space causing lung collapse
rationale: Pneumothorax occurs when air enters the pleural space collapsing the lung. Causes include trauma spontaneous rupture and medical procedures. Tension pneumothorax is life-threatening.

definition: Blood in the pleural space
rationale: Hemothorax is accumulation of blood in the pleural space usually from trauma. It can cause both respiratory compromise from lung compression and hypovolemic shock from blood loss.

definition: Stroke caused by blocked or ruptured brain blood vessel
rationale: CVA or stroke occurs when blood flow to part of the brain is interrupted by clot ischemic or bleeding hemorrhagic. Brain cells die causing neurological deficits. Time is critical for treatment.

definition: Temporary stroke-like symptoms that resolve completely
rationale: TIA is a brief episode of stroke-like symptoms that resolve completely usually within an hour. It is a warning sign that a full stroke may follow and requires urgent evaluation.

definition: Elevated pressure inside the skull
rationale: Increased ICP results from bleeding swelling or mass effect inside the rigid skull. It can compress brain tissue and herniate the brain through openings causing death. Signs include altered consciousness and pupil changes.

definition: Brain tissue pushed through openings in skull due to pressure
rationale: Brain herniation occurs when increased ICP pushes brain tissue through openings in the skull compressing vital structures. It is life-threatening and manifests as declining consciousness and pupil changes.

definition: Period of confusion and recovery following a seizure
rationale: The postictal state follows a seizure characterized by confusion drowsiness and gradual return to baseline. Duration varies from minutes to hours. Patients may not remember the seizure.

definition: Abnormally low blood glucose level
rationale: Hypoglycemia is blood glucose below 70 mg/dL causing symptoms from brain glucose deprivation. Signs include confusion diaphoresis and altered mental status progressing to seizures and coma if untreated.

definition: Abnormally high blood glucose level
rationale: Hyperglycemia is elevated blood glucose typically above 200 mg/dL. It develops gradually and can lead to diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar state. Symptoms include polyuria polydipsia and altered mental status.

definition: Life-threatening complication of diabetes with high glucose and ketones
rationale: DKA occurs primarily in Type 1 diabetes when lack of insulin causes very high glucose and ketone production. Signs include altered mental status Kussmaul respirations fruity breath and dehydration.

definition: Abnormally low blood pH
rationale: Acidosis is blood pH below 7.35 caused by excess acid or loss of base. Respiratory acidosis results from CO2 retention. Metabolic acidosis results from acid production as in DKA or acid ingestion.

definition: Abnormally high blood pH
rationale: Alkalosis is blood pH above 7.45 caused by loss of acid or excess base. Respiratory alkalosis results from hyperventilation blowing off CO2. Metabolic alkalosis can result from vomiting or excess antacids.

definition: Inadequate tissue perfusion
rationale: Shock is failure of the cardiovascular system to adequately perfuse tissues with oxygenated blood. Without treatment it progresses to organ failure and death. Multiple types exist based on cause.

definition: Shock caused by fluid or blood loss
rationale: Hypovolemic shock results from loss of blood or fluid volume reducing cardiac output. Causes include hemorrhage dehydration and burns. Treatment includes stopping bleeding and volume replacement.

definition: Shock caused by heart pump failure
rationale: Cardiogenic shock occurs when the heart cannot pump adequately typically from massive MI or severe arrhythmia. Unlike hypovolemic shock fluid administration may worsen it by overloading the failing heart.

definition: Shock caused by widespread vasodilation
rationale: Distributive shock results from inappropriate vasodilation causing relative hypovolemia. Subtypes include septic anaphylactic and neurogenic shock. Blood volume is normal but vessels are too dilated.

definition: Shock caused by overwhelming infection
rationale: Septic shock is distributive shock caused by systemic infection triggering massive inflammatory response and vasodilation. It has high mortality and requires antibiotics and aggressive supportive care.

definition: Shock caused by severe allergic reaction
rationale: Anaphylactic shock is distributive shock from severe allergic reaction causing massive histamine release vasodilation and bronchospasm. Epinephrine is life-saving treatment reversing both bronchospasm and vasodilation.

definition: Shock caused by spinal cord injury disrupting vascular control
rationale: Neurogenic shock results from spinal cord injury disrupting sympathetic control of blood vessels causing vasodilation. Unlike other shock it presents with bradycardia and warm dry skin below the injury.

definition: Early shock where body maintains blood pressure through compensation
rationale: Compensated shock is early shock where compensatory mechanisms maintain blood pressure. Signs include tachycardia pallor and delayed capillary refill despite normal BP. Recognition allows early intervention.

definition: Late shock where compensation fails and BP drops
rationale: Decompensated shock occurs when compensatory mechanisms fail and blood pressure drops. This is a late and ominous sign indicating imminent cardiovascular collapse. Aggressive intervention is critical.

definition: Bruise caused by blunt trauma
rationale: Contusion is a bruise where blunt force damages blood vessels under intact skin causing bleeding into tissues. It appears as discoloration progressing through color changes as it heals.

definition: Irregular tear in tissue from forceful impact
rationale: Laceration is an irregular wound with torn edges caused by forceful impact against tissue. Edges are jagged unlike incisions which have clean edges. Contamination risk is higher with lacerations.

definition: Tissue torn away from the body
rationale: Avulsion occurs when tissue is forcefully torn away either completely amputated or partially remaining attached by a flap. Bleeding can be severe and reattachment may be possible for some avulsions.

definition: Mild traumatic brain injury from head impact
rationale: Concussion is mild TBI from head trauma causing temporary neurological dysfunction. Symptoms include confusion amnesia headache and balance problems. Most resolve but repeated concussions have cumulative effects.

definition: Bleeding between skull and outer brain covering
rationale: Epidural hematoma is arterial bleeding between the skull and dura mater typically from temporal bone fracture tearing the middle meningeal artery. Classic presentation is brief LOC lucid interval then rapid deterioration.

definition: Bleeding between brain coverings
rationale: Subdural hematoma is venous bleeding between the dura and arachnoid membranes. It may be acute from significant trauma or chronic from minor trauma in elderly. Symptoms develop more slowly than epidural.

definition: Multiple rib fractures creating free-floating chest segment
rationale: Flail chest occurs when multiple ribs are fractured in multiple places creating a segment that moves paradoxically. It impairs ventilation and indicates severe thoracic trauma. Underlying lung contusion is common.

definition: Air trapped under pressure collapsing lung and shifting organs
rationale: Tension pneumothorax occurs when air enters the pleural space through a one-way valve mechanism building pressure. It collapses the lung shifts the mediastinum and compresses the heart causing cardiovascular collapse.

definition: Severe systemic allergic reaction
rationale: Anaphylaxis is a severe rapidly progressive allergic reaction involving multiple body systems. It can cause airway obstruction cardiovascular collapse and death within minutes. Epinephrine is life-saving treatment.

definition: Chemical released during allergic reactions causing symptoms
rationale: Histamine is released from mast cells during allergic reactions causing vasodilation increased permeability bronchospasm and itching. Antihistamines block histamine receptors reducing symptoms.

definition: Swelling of deep tissue especially around face and airway
rationale: Angioedema is swelling in deeper skin layers and mucous membranes often affecting face lips tongue and airway. Unlike hives it involves deeper tissues. Airway angioedema is life-threatening.
